items.q: Did the Bolsheviks overthrow the Tsar in October 1917?
items.correct: No - Nicholas II abdicated during the February Revolution; in October the Bolsheviks overthrew the Provisional Government that had replaced him.
items.misconception: Lenin and the Bolsheviks toppled Tsar Nicholas II in October 1917.
items.random_wrong: The Tsar was overthrown by a German army occupying Petrograd.
items.why: Two revolutions in one year get merged in students' minds; the February and October revolutions removed different governments.

items.q: Was the New Economic Policy a return to full capitalism?
items.correct: No - small trade and peasant markets were allowed, but the state kept control of the 'commanding heights': heavy industry, banking and foreign trade.
items.misconception: The NEP abandoned communism and restored capitalism in Russia.
items.random_wrong: The NEP banned all private trade in the USSR.
items.why: Students read 'allowed private trade' as total reversal; Lenin saw the NEP as a limited, temporary retreat to save the regime.

items.q: Did the Five-Year Plans modernise the USSR without cost?
items.correct: No - output of coal, iron and steel rose dramatically, but figures were often falsified, quality was poor, and workers faced harsh discipline and dangerous conditions.
items.misconception: The Five-Year Plans transformed Soviet industry smoothly and at no human cost.
items.random_wrong: The Five-Year Plans were abandoned after one year.
items.why: Propaganda images of heroic workers hide the coercion, falsified statistics and human suffering that GCSE answers need to weigh against real industrial growth.

items.q: Why did Nicholas II's decision to take personal command of the army in 1915 damage him?
items.correct: He was personally blamed for defeats, while government at home fell under the influence of the Tsarina and Rasputin
items.misconception: It broke an agreement with Germany and triggered invasion
items.random_wrong: The army immediately mutinied and arrested him
items.why: Correct answer: He was personally blamed for defeats, while government at home fell under the influence of the Tsarina and Rasputin. “It broke an agreement with Germany and triggered invasion” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which slogan summed up the Bolshevik promises of 1917?
items.why: It's 'Peace, Bread and Land'. “'Liberty, Equality, Fraternity'” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: The New Economic Policy. “The Five-Year Plans” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was the main aim of Stalin's Five-Year Plans?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Rapid industrialisation of the USSR. “Restoring private ownership of factories” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did the October Revolution occur?
items.why: The real figure is 1917 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
